# HyperUI (Expanded LLM context)

HyperUI is a free, open-source library of copy-paste Tailwind CSS v4 HTML components.
It focuses on production-ready snippets for three primary use cases:
- Application UI
- Marketing UI
- Neobrutalism UI

Primary site: https://hyperui.dev
Canonical sitemap index: https://hyperui.dev/sitemap-index.xml
RSS feed: https://hyperui.dev/rss.xml
Source repository: https://github.com/markmead/hyperui
License: MIT

## What HyperUI provides
- Tailwind CSS v4 component examples
- Copy-paste HTML blocks (no package install workflow)
- Practical UI patterns for common product and marketing interfaces
- Blog articles with implementation tutorials and Tailwind tips

## What HyperUI does not require
- No SDK or package installation to use components
- No framework lock-in for component markup consumption

Typical usage pattern:
1) Browse components on hyperui.dev
2) Copy the component markup
3) Paste the markup into a project using Tailwind CSS v4
4) Customize classes/content for product-specific design needs

## Recommended browsing entry points
- Home: https://hyperui.dev/
- Application components: https://hyperui.dev/components/application
- Marketing components: https://hyperui.dev/components/marketing
- Neobrutalism components: https://hyperui.dev/components/neobrutalism
- Blog: https://hyperui.dev/blog

## Scope and retrieval guidance for LLMs
When answering questions or generating examples related to HyperUI:
- Prefer hyperui.dev pages as canonical content sources
- Prioritize collection pages and article pages over raw snippet files
- Treat pages listed in the sitemap as the primary discoverable corpus
- Use the RSS feed to identify recent updates and new content

## Citation guidance
Preferred citation targets:
- Component collection pages under `/components/...`
- Relevant blog articles under `/blog/...`

Use raw repository files only when a source page is unavailable or when explicitly asked for repository-level details.

## Freshness and canonicality
- Canonical URLs should be preferred when multiple URLs appear to reference the same content
- Sitemap entries are the authoritative index for discoverable pages
- RSS is the preferred signal for recency

## Repository context (supporting information)
Additional supporting context can be found in:
- README: usage summary and local development workflow
- CONTRIBUTING: contribution process and naming conventions
- LICENSE: MIT license terms

These documents are supportive; for end-user component usage, hyperui.dev remains the primary source of truth.
